one_line_takeaway: VLMShield pairs a CLIP-based long-text/cross-modal feature extractor (MAFE) with a lightweight 3-layer classifier to give VLMs a plug-and-play input filter that cuts jailbreak ASR to near-zero (0.00-2.13% ASR across in- and out-of-domain benchmarks) while adding only 0.34s of latency, though white-box adaptive dilution/optimization attacks still achieve up to ~3.8% effective ASR.
key_finding: On LLaVA-1.5-13B, VLMShield achieves 0.19% ASR on in-domain image-based jailbreaks and 0.00% on in-domain text-based jailbreaks (JailbreakV-28K), versus internal baselines ASTRA (5.21% ASR) and VLMGuard (16.37% ASR), and versus external baselines ranging from 17.19% (MirrorCheck) up to 80.04% (SelfReminder) on the same image-based-jailbreak IND column. Out-of-domain, VLMShield holds 0.00% ASR on FigStep, 2.13% on HADES, 0.41% on AdvBench-M, 0.71% on MM-SafetyBench, and 1.62% on VLSafe (maximum degradation from IND to OOD is 2.13 percentage points). On benign utility, VLMShield reaches 100% accuracy on in-domain benign sets (GPT4V-Caption, CC3M) and 96.33% (MM-Vet) / 99.84% (MMBench) OOD, versus baseline drops as low as 89.41% (MirrorCheck) and 89.04% (ECSO) on some OOD benign sets. On efficiency, VLMShield adds only 0.34s detection overhead (identical for both VLMs tested), yielding total end-to-end latency of 8.36s (LLaVA) / 4.20s (Qwen2.5-VL), a 4.2%/8.8% increase over base generation time (8.02s/3.86s); by contrast JailGuard's mutation-based detection alone costs 291.48s (LLaVA) / 208.05s (Qwen), and other external baselines run 6-8x slower detection than VLMShield. Under white-box adaptive attacks (RQ5), optimizing purely to evade VLMShield (lambda=1) drives up ASR but collapses actual harm: text-based attacks reach 4.47% ASR but only 18.09% HGR (0.81% effective ASR); image-based reach 5.04% ASR but 13.17% HGR (0.66% effective ASR); combined text+image attacks peak at 2.06% effective ASR at lambda=0.5; dilution attacks at a 1:100 benign:malicious chunk ratio reach 8.73% ASR but HGR falls to 43.8% (3.82% effective ASR). The paper's own ablations (RQ4) show similarity-weighted chunk aggregation (0.00% ASR, MMD 0.835) outperforms simple averaging (1.46% ASR, MMD 0.692) and max-pooling (5.39% ASR, MMD 0.507), and that a 0.5 classification threshold balances 96.33% benign accuracy against 0.00% ASR, while thresholds of 0.3/0.4 leak 10.04%/5.27% ASR and thresholds of 0.6/0.7 drop benign accuracy to 90.46%/83.84%.
core_contribution: The paper's own contributions (not related work) are: (1) MAFE (Multimodal Aggregated Feature Extraction), a CLIP-based framework that overcomes CLIP's 77-token text limit via progressive text aggregation (splitting text into overlapping 75-token chunks, extracting [EOS] embeddings per chunk, and combining them via similarity-weighted aggregation where each chunk's weight is its average cosine similarity to all other chunks) and fuses this with the image [CLS] embedding via concatenation into a 1536-d joint representation; (2) an empirical finding that MAFE-extracted features show clear distributional separation (measured via t-SNE, PCA, and Maximum Mean Discrepancy) between benign prompts and three malicious categories (direct malicious, image-based jailbreak, text-based jailbreak); (3) VLMShield, a lightweight 3-layer fully-connected network (1536->1024->512->2, ReLU, dropout p=0.5, softmax output) trained as a binary classifier on MAFE features to act as a plug-and-play pre-filter that blocks malicious multimodal prompts before they reach the VLM, requiring no VLM retraining or internal access.
threat_model: External, black-box, plug-and-play defense positioned in front of a VLM (evaluated with LLaVA-1.5-13B and Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct). Attackers submit multimodal (image+text) prompts intended to elicit harmful VLM outputs, via three attack families: direct malicious prompts (explicit harmful content, e.g. MM-SafetyBench, VLSafe), image-based jailbreaks (e.g. FigStep typographic prompts, HADES adversarial-perturbation jailbreaks), and text-based jailbreaks (e.g. JailbreakV-28K, AdvBench-M). The paper additionally studies an adaptive/white-box variant in RQ5, where the attacker has full knowledge of VLMShield's architecture, MAFE feature representations, and detection threshold, and must craft inputs (GCG-optimized text suffixes, HADES image perturbations, combined and 'dilution' attacks that bury malicious content within large amounts of benign context, exploiting MAFE's chunk-averaging) to jointly evade detection and remain harmful, since the [EOS]/[CLS]-to-feature transform is non-invertible so attackers cannot directly manipulate the feature vector.
limitations: The paper itself does not devote a dedicated limitations section; limitations must be inferred from its own reported results and design. (1) Adaptive attacks are not fully neutralized -- effective ASR under white-box optimization ranges up to ~3.8% at extreme dilution ratios (100 benign chunks per malicious chunk), and combined text+image attacks reach 2.06% effective ASR, i.e. non-zero residual risk against attackers with full knowledge of the defense. (2) The detector's benign-vs-malicious classification is a binary decision with a tunable threshold, and the paper's own ablation shows an explicit accuracy/robustness trade-off (higher thresholds reduce ASR but also cut benign accuracy to as low as 83.84% at threshold 0.7). (3) The training/evaluation malicious data is dominated by JailbreakV-28K-derived sources for training, and while OOD generalization is tested, all OOD malicious categories tested (FigStep, HADES, AdvBench-M, MM-SafetyBench, VLSafe) are still established public benchmarks rather than genuinely novel unseen attack families. (4) MAFE's chunk-based aggregation, which enables long-text handling, is itself the mechanism exploited by the dilution attack strategy in RQ5, indicating an inherent tension between the design's efficiency mechanism and its adversarial robustness.

datasets_used: JailbreakV-28K - image-based and text-based jailbreak (IND, held-out 20% split)
quantitative_results: IND ASR (LLaVA-1.5-13B): 0.19% image-based jailbreak, 0.00% text-based jailbreak (JailbreakV-28K)
OOD ASR (LLaVA-1.5-13B): 0.00% FigStep, 2.13% HADES, 0.41% AdvBench-M, 0.71% MM-SafetyBench, 1.62% VLSafe
Benign ACC: 100% IND (GPT4V-Caption, CC3M), 96.33% MM-Vet, 99.84% MMBench (both LLaVA and Qwen)
Detection overhead: 0.34s (both VLMs); total latency 8.36s (LLaVA) / 4.20s (Qwen2.5-VL), a 4.2%/8.8% increase over base generation (8.02s/3.86s)
Adaptive white-box attacks (lambda=1): text-based 4.47% ASR / 18.09% HGR / 0.81% effective ASR; image-based 5.04% ASR / 13.17% HGR / 0.66% effective ASR
Combined text+image adaptive attack: max effective ASR 2.06% at lambda=0.5
Dilution attack at 100:1 chunk ratio: 8.73% ASR, 43.8% HGR, 3.82% effective ASR
MMD (feature separation) benign vs. image jailbreak 0.866, benign vs. text jailbreak 0.906, benign vs. direct malicious 0.746, image vs. text jailbreak 1.000
Ablation: similarity-weighted aggregation 0.00% ASR (MMD 0.835) vs. simple averaging 1.46% ASR (MMD 0.692) vs. max-pooling 5.39% ASR (MMD 0.507)
Ablation: threshold 0.5 -> 96.33% benign ACC / 0.00% ASR; threshold 0.3/0.4 -> 10.04%/5.27% ASR; threshold 0.6/0.7 -> 90.46%/83.84% benign ACC
baselines_compared: ASTRA (internal/white-box, activation steering) - 5.21% IND image-jailbreak ASR
VLMGuard (internal/white-box, PCA-based anomaly detection) - 16.37% IND image-jailbreak ASR
JailGuard (external, mutation-based consistency) - 22.05% IND image-jailbreak ASR, 291.48s detection latency on LLaVA
CIDER (external, denoising-based image-text similarity) - 37.20% IND image-jailbreak ASR
MirrorCheck (external, denoised-image embedding comparison) - 17.19% IND image-jailbreak ASR
SelfReminder (external, protective system prompt) - 80.04% IND image-jailbreak ASR
ECSO (external, output monitoring/regeneration) - 39.68% IND image-jailbreak ASR
